vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's minlen parameter controls requirements for minimum characters required in a password. Add minlen= after pam_pwquality to set minimum password length requirements.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_minlen='' if grep -sq minlen /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/minlen/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^minlen") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_minlen" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -q -m

triage_decision.reason: The setting adjusts the minimum password length via pam_pwquality which is a standard STIG hardening parameter; modifications are typically safe and do not affect SSH connectivity or system stability.

review_feedback: The remediation script is incomplete, uses undefined variables, lacks validation and backup, and has not been executed, so it does not yet resolve the vulnerability. Suggestions: Define and validate the target minlen value (e.g., target_minlen=12) and ensure it is a positive integer.; Create backups of /etc/security/pwquality.conf and any *.conf files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d before modification.; Use a precise sed pattern to remove existing minlen lines, avoiding accidental deletion of unrelated entries.; Append the minlen entry only if it does not already exist, then verify the syntax with a config parser or pwquality‑validate.; Explicitly reload or restart the relevant PAM‑using service (e.g., systemctl restart sshd) only after confirming successful reload.; Add error handling and exit codes to make the script idempotent and auditable.; Consider using pam-auth-update or a package‑provided mechanism to apply password‑quality settings safely.

concerns: The remediation script is incomplete – it references undefined variables (e.g., var_password_pam_minlen) and uses non‑existent commands such as pam_auth({type=systemd}).
No validation is performed on the desired minlen value, and there is no backup of modified configuration files.
The reload step is only described, not executed, and depends on the init system; a more robust approach would use pam-auth-update or similar.
suggested_improvements: Define and validate the target minlen value before writing the file.
Create a backup of any edited pwquality.conf or drop‑in files.
Use a dedicated script that safely appends or replaces minlen using sed with proper quoting, and verify the resulting syntax with pwquality‑validate or a config parser.
Explicitly reload or restart the relevant PAM service (e.g., `systemctl restart sshd` or `pam-auth-update`) to ensure changes take effect.
Add error handling and exit codes to make the script idempotent and auditable.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation script is incomplete, uses undefined variables, lacks validation and backup, and has not been executed, so it does not yet resolve the vulnerability.
concerns: Uses non‑existent commands such as pam_auth({type=systemd}).
No validation of the target minlen value before writing to config files.
No backup of original configuration files before modification.
Cleanup of existing minlen lines is not robust; regex may miss variations.
Appending to drop‑in files without checking for existing entries may cause duplication.
Service reload/restart described but not executed; risk of breaking SSH if done incorrectly.
No idempotent handling of errors or exit codes, making the process non‑auditable.
suggested_improvements: Define and validate the target minlen value (e.g., target_minlen=12) and ensure it is a positive integer.
Create backups of /etc/security/pwquality.conf and any *.conf files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d before modification.
Use a precise sed pattern to remove existing minlen lines, avoiding accidental deletion of unrelated entries.
Append the minlen entry only if it does not already exist, then verify the syntax with a config parser or pwquality‑validate.
Explicitly reload or restart the relevant PAM‑using service (e.g., systemctl restart sshd) only after confirming successful reload.
Add error handling and exit codes to make the script idempotent and auditable.
Consider using pam-auth-update or a package‑provided mechanism to apply password‑quality settings safely.
